text: Upon entering the abdomen, the initial view reveals flecks of tissue and fluid accumulation in the right subhepatic and subdiaphragmatic areas. The surgeon performs initial exploration and suction to remove the fluid.
After initial exploration, the surgeon observes a greater amount of tissue flakes and fluid, focusing on the possible site of perforation near the first part of the duodenum.
The surgeon traces the antrum of the stomach within the abdominal cavity, visually inspecting the area for any signs of perforation or abnormalities.
The surgeon traces the entron and proceeds to the anterior surface of the first part of the duodenum, preparing for further exploration or repair.
The surgeon visually identifies a small perforation on the anterior surface of the first part of the duodenum, with gastric contents visibly leaking from the site.
The surgeon exposes the red mucosa of the duodenum, highlighting the area of interest for ulcer repair.
The surgeon identifies the area of perforation on the anterior surface of the first part of the duodenum, where gastric contents are visibly escaping.

The surgeon clears the surrounding flakes and trims the margin of the perforation on the anterior surface of the duodenum. Tissue near the perforated area is visibly excised and prepared for biopsy and histopathological examination.
The surgeon clears flakes around the perforation area and places three stay sutures using 2-0 PDS, following the technical aspects of Graham's repair during laparoscopic surgery.
The surgeon places three stay sutures: one on the proximal margin of the duodenal perforation, one through the middle of the perforation, and one on the lower part of the perforation.
The surgeon ensures that all suture bites pass completely through the tissue layers surrounding the perforation site.

The surgeon takes a thick, deep bite with the suture needle through the tissue surrounding the perforation, ensuring the suture holds securely after closing the defect. The sutures are divided and prepared for the next bite.
The instrument inserted through the right anterior axillary port is used for liver retraction, providing clear exposure of the surgical field to facilitate taking precise suturing bites.
The surgeon takes the third full-thickness bite through the defect using a suture, ensuring the stitch passes completely through the tissue layers of the hole.
The surgeon has placed three stay sutures over the defect area, ensuring they sit securely across the ulcer site.
The surgeon selects a well-vascularized and mobile segment of the omentum to cover the defect, ensuring it is positioned to provide adequate coverage.

The surgeon places the omentum over the defect area, with the assistant holding the tip of the omentum in position. The surgeon then places the most proximal suture, securing the first bite and knot to hold the omentum in place.

The surgeon positions the omentum over the defect area, ensuring it remains stable after the first suture is secured. The assistant releases the omentum, preventing instrument crowding and allowing for better mobility. The instrument used for liver retraction is removed after securing the first knot.
The surgeon places additional sutures to secure the omentum over the defect area, ensuring it is held in place by the newly placed nodes.
After securing the knot, the surgeon divides the excess suture and prepares to take the second bite. The suture beyond the defect is tightened to ensure it remains secure.
The surgeon ensures that the third knot is securely tied over the omentum, preventing it from slipping through the sutures as the omentum covers the defect area.
The surgeon carefully adjusts the pressure applied to the omentum while securing sutures, ensuring that the tension does not compromise the blood supply to the omental tissue.
After placing all the sutures, the surgeon performs a thorough peritoneal lavage, visibly irrigating the abdominal cavity to cleanse the area around the repaired ulcer site.
The surgeon stands at the head end side while the camera assistant positions on the left side of the surgeon, facilitating the peritoneal lavage in the pelvic region.
After completing the peritoneal lavage, the surgeon prepares to place a surgical drain in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon observes that the bowels appear unduly dilated, a common occurrence in cases of perforated peritonitis due to induced paralytic ileus.
The surgeon encounters dilated small bowel loops, which may visually obstruct the process of peritoneal lavage due to bowel retraction.
The surgeon places two drains after completing the lavage: one in the hepatorenal pouch in the sub-hepatic area through the right anterior axillary port.
The surgeon inserts a 24 French drain through the left pararectal 5mm port into the pelvis, completing the procedure.
